tier: Semantic vs. Instance vs. Panoptic Segmentation
title: What is Image Segmentation?
concept: Image segmentation classifies images at the individual pixel level, drawing precise boundaries instead of coarse bounding boxes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main output of an object detection model?

options.text: A single classification label for the whole image
A continuous scalar value like house price

exercises.explain: Correct! Object detection finds objects and draws boxes around them, whereas segmentation goes pixel-by-pixel.

cards.title: Beyond bounding boxes
cards.body: Imagine a self-driving car seeing a pedestrian. A box tells us where they generally are, but not their exact shape. What if the pedestrian is leaning over? To avoid accidents, the AI needs to know exactly which pixels belong to the person and which belong to the road.

cards.title: Image Segmentation
cards.body: Image Segmentation is the process of classifying every single pixel in an image into a category. Instead of drawing a box, we paint a precise mask over the object, separating it from the background.

cards.title: Coloring book vs. Sticky notes
cards.body: Object detection is like putting sticky notes on a page to point out where things are. Image segmentation is like carefully coloring inside the lines of a coloring book, painting every object its own distinct color.

cards.title: Self-driving lane detection
cards.body: A self-driving car's camera captures the road. Rather than drawing boxes around lanes, a segmentation model colors the lane pixels blue, the drivable road green, and sidewalks purple, giving the car a pixel-perfect map.

cards.title: Not just detection
cards.body: You might think segmentation is just detection with extra steps. But it's fundamentally different: detection locates objects with boxes, while segmentation understands the exact boundary and contour of objects, handling irregular shapes easily.

cards.title: Detection vs. Segmentation
cards.body: How they differ in output precision:

left.text:
Outputs rectangles (bounding boxes)
Less precise boundaries
Faster, lower detail

right.text:
Outputs pixel masks
Exact object boundaries
Higher detail, more compute

exercises.prompt: What is the core objective of image segmentation?

options.text: To classify every individual pixel in an image into a category
To draw a rectangular box around every object
To predict a single class label for the entire image
To compress the image size for faster web loading

exercises.prompt: True or False: Image segmentation models output rectangular bounding boxes.
exercises.explain: False. Bounding boxes are for object detection. Segmentation outputs precise pixel-level masks.

exercises.sentence: While object detection draws boxes, image segmentation predicts class labels for every ___, creating a pixel-perfect mask.
exercises.explain: Image segmentation works at the pixel level to classify every single pixel.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a classic image segmentation task?

options.text: Identifying the exact pixel boundaries of a tumor in an X-ray
Deciding if an image contains a dog or a cat
Counting the number of cars in a parking lot using boxes

exercises.explain: Segmenting a tumor requires pixel-precise boundaries, making it a segmentation task.

exercises.prompt: A robot arm needs to pick up irregularly shaped, overlapping puzzle pieces. Why is image segmentation better than object detection here?

options.text: Segmentation provides the exact pixel boundaries, helping the robot grab the piece at its precise contour without bumping others.
Segmentation is always faster and uses less memory on robot computers.
Object detection cannot recognize colors, but segmentation can.
Detection boxes are too small to fit the puzzle pieces.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The precise contours provided by segmentation prevent collisions and ensure a accurate grip. Next, let's look at the different types of segmentation!
